elements.sent-2411-004: Smart grids feature a bidirectional flow of electricity and data, enhancing flexibility, efficiency, and reliability in increasingly volatile energy grids.
elements.sent-2411-005: However, data from smart meters can reveal sensitive private information.
elements.sent-2411-006: Consequently, the adoption of smart meters is often restricted via legal means and hampered by limited user acceptance.
elements.sent-2411-007: Since metering data is beneficial for fault-free grid operation, power management, and resource allocation, applying privacy-preserving techniques to smart metering data is an important research problem.
elements.sent-2411-008: This work addresses this by using secure multi-party computation (SMPC), which allows multiple parties to jointly evaluate functions of their private inputs without revealing the latter.
elements.sent-2411-009: Concretely, we show how to perform power flow analysis on cryptographically hidden prosumer data.
elements.sent-2411-010: More precisely, we present a tailored solution to the power flow problem building on an SMPC implementation of Newton's method.
elements.sent-2411-011: We analyze the security of our approach in the universal composability framework and provide benchmarks for various grid types, threat models, and solvers.
elements.sent-2411-012: Our results indicate that secure multi-party computation can be able to alleviate privacy issues in smart grids in certain applications.
elements.sent-2411-013: Unfortunately, unlike traditional sources, they introduce uncertainty regarding power generation and hence, flexible energy sources (e.g., costly secondary power plants) are currently necessary to ensure balanced loads.
elements.sent-2411-014: Ideally, the power feed-in of renewables would not impair the grid's reliability and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-015: In this context, power flow analysis (PFA) based on power forecasts can help to contain issues such as voltage instability, overloads, and reactive power requirements \cite{ajjarapu1992continuation by providing crucial information about the power system's performance.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Here, usage data is a key enabler because it serves as a basis for such forecasts and more reliable planning.
elements.sent-2411-017: Especially low-voltage networks would benefit from prosumer data since the massive deployment of photovoltaics and batteries has a significant impact on load balances.
elements.sent-2411-018: Smart meters form an essential building block in smart grids by providing prosumer data in the form of voltage, current, and power.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, it is very simple to estimate household occupancy or the number of residents based on this data.
elements.sent-2411-020: With more advanced methods, it has been shown that it is possible to classify electrical devices in use or even which TV channel is watched \cite{asghar2017smart,Molina2010private,Greveler2012multimedia,WOOD2003821,5054916 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Such information is already a valuable resource that can be exploited for burglary, mass surveillance, or tailored advertisement \cite{jawurek2012privacy .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Furthermore, detailed private information can be disclosed when additional background knowledge is available \cite{Molina2010private .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Thus, leakage of smart meter data poses a major threat.
elements.sent-2411-024: At the same time, private companies (with or without regulatory oversight) are partially commissioned to store and process this data.
elements.sent-2411-025: For instance, Itron, Triliant, Landis+Gyr, Oracle, Sensus, and Siemens offer storage and processing solutions for metering data.
elements.sent-2411-026: Besides regulatory measures, technical solutions can help to secure smart metering data against abuse.
elements.sent-2411-027: Hence, institutions such as the European Commission and the US National Institute for Standards and Technology stress the importance of introducing privacy-preserving measures to smart grids \cite{EUcommission15 \cite{NISTIR7628 .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Against this background, we are interested in a privacy-preserving PFA based on confidential smart metering and forecast data.
elements.sent-2411-029: This way, a reliable grid operation and security are no longer conflicting goals.
elements.sent-2411-030: Our interest in power flow is additionally driven by its fundamental role in power systems and the fact that solution techniques via Newton's method (which we will use here) are applied across various contexts.
elements.sent-2411-031: Specifically, these methods are pivotal in state estimation, optimal power flow \cite[Chapters 2.6 and 8]{zhu2015optimization , and advanced grid optimization techniques \cite{faulwasser2018optimal , underscoring their relevance beyond the scope of this paper.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Privacy-preservation in smart grids has been addressed in the literature before.
elements.sent-2411-033: We compactly summarize related works and point out conceptual differences to our approach in \Cref{tab:related .}
elements.sent-2411-034: The listed works can be distinguished based on the applied cryptographic technique, the application, whether a trusted third party (TTP) is required, and whether some crucial data is leaked.
elements.sent-2411-035: We also provide a coarse classification of the problem complexity, which reflects the difficulty of implementing the task at hand securely, without making use of a TTP or leaking data.
elements.sent-2411-036: In the following, we discuss some related works~in~more~detail.
elements.sent-2411-037: Common attempts to alleviate privacy problems are restricting the measurement periods to $15$ minutes or more, limiting the data storage duration, and anonymization \cite{efthymiou2010smart .}
elements.sent-2411-038: However, these methods prevent leveraging all available information, require trust, and provide limited security guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-039: Conceivable cryptographic techniques with robust security guarantees are differential privacy (DP), homomorphic encryption (HE), and secure multi-party computation (SMPC).
elements.sent-2411-040: The main goal of these techniques is to support some sort of function evaluation while maintaining the privacy of the function's inputs.
elements.sent-2411-041: In DP, carefully constructed noise is added to the data, to hide information while some computation is still possible.
elements.sent-2411-042: Consequently, a trade-off between accuracy and security arises.

elements.sent-2411-057: When comparing the available frameworks for SMPC and HE, it becomes clear that those for SMPC are more mature than their counterparts for HE.
elements.sent-2411-058: In addition, due to the heavy computational overhead of HE, SMPC performs much better in settings where the computing parties consist of resource-constrained devices such as smart meters.
elements.sent-2411-059: Thus, in this work, we present the first realization of a fully privacy-preserving PFA based on Newton's method, leveraging the strong security guarantees of SMPC protocols.
elements.sent-2411-060: In more detail, the main contributions of our paper can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-061: While SMPC allows for arbitrary computations, a careless choice of protocols and parameters or naive implementations of algorithms typically lead to inefficient results.
elements.sent-2411-062: We provide an efficient SMPC implementation tailored for PFA by considering a suitable Cartesian formulation and by exploiting sparsity.
elements.sent-2411-063: Additionally, we adapt solvers for systems of linear equations (such as LU decomposition and GMRES) and integrate line search techniques.
elements.sent-2411-064: We share our implementation for reproducibility.
elements.sent-2411-065: We provide a security analysis of our algorithm in the universal composability framework (UC) \cite{Canetti01 .}
elements.sent-2411-066: This is especially important since PFA is an important subtask in applications such as flexibility markets, demand response, detection of voltage range violations, state estimation, and optimal power flow.
elements.sent-2411-067: UC-security means that our algorithm can be securely used as a component of other secure programs, e.g., for aforementioned complex applications.
elements.sent-2411-068: We benchmark our algorithm (using both LU and GMRES solvers) with several SMPC protocols for two grids of varied topology and size.
elements.sent-2411-069: In addition, we break out runtime by network latency.
elements.sent-2411-070: Since the utilized SMPC protocols provide differing security guarantees, we can also show the efficiency of our algorithm for various threat models.
elements.sent-2411-071: This enables us to present profound insights into the practicality of SMPC in the context of PFA, and allows us to substantiate the claim that SMPC-based privacy-preserving PFA can be practical for many threat models and real-world settings.

elements.sent-2411-081: Power systems are typically modeled by a set of buses (nodes) and branches (transmission lines).
elements.sent-2411-082: Remarkably, assuming stationary operating conditions in a grid with alternating current, the $N\in\N$ buses are fully described by the voltages $\vb\in\C^{N}$ (typically given in terms of magnitude and phase angle) as well as the active and reactive powers $\pb\in\R^N$ and $\qb\in\R^N$.
elements.sent-2411-083: In this work, we focus on the privacy of smart grid prosumers.
elements.sent-2411-084: Therefore, only load buses (PQ-buses) are of interest here.

elements.sent-2411-086: In order to find $\vb$, we first note that it is linearly related to the currents $\ib\in\C^{N}$ by the admittance matrix $\Yb=\Gb+\jc\Bb$ via $\ib=\Yb\vb$.
elements.sent-2411-087: With this relationship, the bus powers can be equivalently and compactly expressed via the apparent power as $\sb =\diag(\vb)(\Yb\vb)^\ast$.
elements.sent-2411-088: Finally, with help of the prescribed values $\sb_0=\pb_0 + \jc\qb_0$, we find the relation \begin{equation}\label{eq:powerflow} \sb(\vb)-\sb_0=\diag(\vb)(\Yb\vb)^\ast-\pb_0-\jc\qb_0=\zerob,\end{equation} which is the well-known power flow equation.

elements.sent-2411-097: In SMPC, $n$ parties (sometimes also called players) $\P_1,\dots,\P_n$ agree to jointly compute a function $\yb=\gb(\zb)$, where $\yb = (y_1,\ldots,y_m)^\top$ and $\zb = (z_1,\ldots,z_n)^\top$ with $m,n\in\N$.
elements.sent-2411-098: Here, $z_i$ denotes $\P_i$'s secret input and $\yb$ a vector of outputs.
elements.sent-2411-099: The function $\gb$ is said to be computed securely if the following two conditions are satisfied:.
elements.sent-2411-100: Correctness: The correct result $\yb$ is computed.
elements.sent-2411-101: Privacy: None of the parties obtains any new information about $\zb$, other than what can be inferred from $\yb$.
elements.sent-2411-102: Up to $n-1$ parties may collude to try to learn about the secrets of other parties.
elements.sent-2411-103: We model this by assuming that an adversary that tries to learn about the secret inputs may corrupt up to $t$ of $n$ parties.
elements.sent-2411-104: In the cases $t\leq \floor{n/2}$ and $t\leq n-1$, we say that we assume an honest majority or a dishonest majority of parties, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-105: Depending on the threat model, corrupted parties may either be.
elements.sent-2411-106: Note that the adversary may pool the information gathered by corrupted parties together and (in the malicious model) coordinate the protocol deviations of corrupted parties to learn more information.
elements.sent-2411-107: In our context, the role of the parties $\P_1,\ldots,\P_n$ can be assigned to the prosumers, while the prosumers' secret power data is collected in $\zb$.
elements.sent-2411-108: However, revealing the node voltages $\vb$ would clearly breach the prosumers' privacy.
elements.sent-2411-109: Thus, the output $\yb$ must represent a less vulnerable result, for instance checking operating conditions securely via \eqref{eq:constraints .}

elements.sent-2411-305: We can see from the benchmark data that grid size and network latency have a significant impact on the runtime of our privacy-preserving PFA.
elements.sent-2411-306: If we assume that the prosumers are connected in a LAN via Ethernet, where RTTs of $\qty{1 {\ms}$ are realistic, runtimes of under 30 seconds (for the online phase) and four minutes (for the entire computation, i.e., preprocessing and online phase) are possible in small grids with 13 prosumers.}
elements.sent-2411-307: If prosumers are expected to deviate from the protocol, security against malicious behavior is required.
elements.sent-2411-308: Here, runtimes of one minute and under three hours are possible in the online phase and for the entire computation, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-309: As we increase latency and grid size, our measured runtime increases so that in a grid with 39 prosumers online computation is finished in approximately two minutes, but preprocessing and online phase together take at least 70 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-310: As described in more detail below, the expensive preprocessing phase can, however, be omitted under certain assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-311: Many smart meters today communicate via unoptimized Ethernet, broadband over power lines (BPL) or LTE connections characterized by relatively high RTTs in the range of $\qty{20 {\milli\second}$ to $\qty{150 {\milli\second}$, which would render our solution impractical.}}
elements.sent-2411-312: Assuming an optimized setup, however, RTTs of $1$ ms for LAN-based connections, and $5$ ms for WAN-based connections between smart meters would be possible.
elements.sent-2411-313: For a holistic assessment of practicality, one should also consider hardware constraints: We used a high-performance server for our benchmarks, on which 13 resp.~39 virtual machines (each representing one prosumer) shared 16 \qty{3.5 {\giga\hertz Ryzen 9 cores.}}
elements.sent-2411-314: Nevertheless, the use of more resource-constrained processors usually integrated into smart meters should not be a problem, at least in the online phase of protocols secure against semi-honest adversaries.
elements.sent-2411-315: This is because in these cases, even with a short RTT of \qty{1 {\milli\second , time spent on communication dominates CPU time by a factor of ten.}}
elements.sent-2411-316: With longer RTTs such as \qty{10 {\milli\second , time spent on communication can dominate CPU time by a factor of 100.}}
elements.sent-2411-317: Moreover, RAM load was roughly \qty{0.5 {\giga\byte to \qty{0.75 {\giga\byte per prosumer during the online phase.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-318: While this is slightly more than typically available in smart meters, it should be noted that our protocols can still be optimized for memory usage.
elements.sent-2411-319: Moreover, the \qty{0.5 {\giga\byte includes system overhead, which is likely going to be much lower in the operating systems deployed in smart meters.}}
elements.sent-2411-320: To allow on-device preprocessing and protocols secure in the malicious model, however, hardware improvements to smart meters might be necessary.
elements.sent-2411-321: In addition, even given sufficiently powerful hardware, generation of correlated randomness might take too long to complete offline, especially in use cases with only a small idle period between computations.
elements.sent-2411-322: For these use cases, it may make sense to outsource the generation and distribution of correlated randomness to a trusted dealer.
elements.sent-2411-323: As long as this dealer is oblivious to the communication transmitted during the online phase, it will learn nothing about the secret inputs.
elements.sent-2411-324: In extension, this means that we also have to assume that none of the prosumers is colluding with the dealer.
elements.sent-2411-325: We can reduce the trust required in the dealer by using a setup where the correlated randomness is generated interactively by two non-colluding dealers (e.g., the grid operator and a governmental regulator) such that both dealers only ever see partial shares ('shares of shares') of the correlated randomness assigned to a certain prosumer.
elements.sent-2411-326: Both dealers distribute their partial shares to the respective prosumers, who can then reconstruct their full shares of the correlated randomness from them.
elements.sent-2411-327: This would allow outsourcing of the expensive preprocessing phase.
elements.sent-2411-328: In this work, we investigated the practicality of SMPC techniques for privacy-preserving PFA in the context of smart grids.
elements.sent-2411-329: To this end, we devised a power flow solution algorithm implemented a privacy-preserving PFA, and provided runtime benchmarks for various SMPC protocols, grid parameters, and threat models.
elements.sent-2411-330: Further, we optimized the efficiency of our implementation through various means such as batching of communication rounds and enabling lower precision by dynamic rescaling.
elements.sent-2411-331: Our method is particularly suited for market-based and preventive smart grid applications where prosumer data is used.
elements.sent-2411-332: In these use cases, PFA is performed as part of a grid state forecast to detect grid congestions at an early stage.
elements.sent-2411-333: To solve these grid congestions, market-based solutions like local flexibility markets \cite{Amicarelli17}\cite{Esmat18 , preventive control concepts, or dynamic grid charges are possible.}
elements.sent-2411-334: Since the lead times of these solutions leave between fifteen minutes and one day for running PFA, our privacy-preserving solution is well-suited here.
elements.sent-2411-335: On the other hand, we have shorter lead times of ten seconds to one minute in real-time smart grid control systems, and currently, our implementation can only accommodate these runtimes for small grids with a short RTT.
elements.sent-2411-336: Real-time smart grid applications might hence be addressed in future research.
elements.sent-2411-337: Due to the availability of better tooling, we focused on SMPC via secret sharing in this work.
elements.sent-2411-338: However, it would also be possible to achieve SMPC by means of threshold fully homomorphic encryption where the secret key is distributed among many parties such that the input of all of those parties is required for decryption.
elements.sent-2411-339: In this setting, all parties would encrypt their secret inputs and send the encryptions to a semi-honest server that will perform the computation.
elements.sent-2411-340: Once the function has been evaluated, all parties get the output ciphertext and partake in the decryption.
elements.sent-2411-341: The advantages of this approach are that the communication load is low and that the computational load can be securely outsourced to a high-performance server, potentially allowing for shorter runtimes.
elements.sent-2411-342: Another direction for future research is to explore alternative methods for solving the power flow problem.
elements.sent-2411-343: In this context, the holomorphic embedding load flow method~\cite{Trias2012 stands out due to its guaranteed convergence to the operable correct solution.}
elements.sent-2411-344: Essentially, this is realized by replacing complex voltage variables by a holomorphic function, which results in a solution algorithm that may be well aligned with SMPC protocols.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran .}
